explicit_text: I was living in Rossford, Ohio, with my parents and six siblings before I entered the service. It was a small town where I attended Rossford High School and later met several guys from there while serving in Japan. My family was large, and my brothers had already served in the military by the time I joined. I often hitchhiked back home to Rossford on weekends while I was stationed at Camp Atterbury.

My family was large, and my brothers had already served in the military by the time I joined. I often hitchhiked back home on weekends while I was stationed at a military camp in Indiana.
cues: Rossford High School classmates in Japan

explicit_text: My dad sent me to Toledo, Ohio, to attend the Macomber Vocational School because he wanted me to learn a skill. Toledo was where I spent my tenth and eleventh grades learning auto mechanics through a combination of classroom and shop work. This experience prepared me for a trade before I joined the Army in 1946.

explicit_text: I attended the Macomber Vocational School in Toledo, Ohio, for my tenth and eleventh grades. The school had a unique system where we alternated two weeks of classroom education with two weeks of shop work or apprenticeship in auto mechanics. It was a great way to learn a trade, and even when I didn’t have a job, I could stay in the school shop to keep training.

explicit_text: I joined the United States Army in August 1946 when I was just 16 years old, with my parents' permission. I was inducted at Camp Atterbury in Indianapolis and spent six weeks recovering from a foot injury there before going to basic training at Fort Eustis. I was part of the Transportation Corps and served alongside friends from my hometown and others I met during training and deployment.

explicit_text: My friend and I decided to join the Army in 1946 because the GI Bill was about to expire at the end of that year. We wanted to take advantage of the benefits it offered, especially for college education after our service. That was a big motivation for us to enlist early, even though I was only 16 at the time.

We wanted to take advantage of the benefits it offered, especially for college education after our service. That was a big motivation for us to enlist early, even though I was only 16 at the time.

explicit_text: I was supposed to graduate from Rossford High School in June 1947, but I joined the Army before my senior year. Many of the guys I later met in Japan had graduated from Rossford High School that year, most of them being draftees or enlistees like me. It was a close-knit community, and we would meet up on weekends even while overseas.

explicit_text: Initially, I considered joining the Navy, but their two-year enlistment required bringing your parents to the recruiting station, which I thought wouldn’t work well for me. After trying that route, my friend and I decided to switch to the Army, which had an 18-month enlistment. So I never actually served in the Navy but considered it briefly before joining the Army.

explicit_text: I was inducted into the Army at Camp Atterbury in Indianapolis, Indiana, in August 1946. I spent about six weeks there recovering in Wakeman General Hospital after crushing my right big toe with a piano during a detail. While at Camp Atterbury, I often hitchhiked home to Rossford on weekends, and the camp was a first-class military base with regular barracks.

explicit_text: Indianapolis was where Camp Atterbury was located, and that's where I was inducted into the Army. I spent my first six weeks of service there, including time in the hospital. I also hitchhiked between Indianapolis and my hometown of Rossford on weekends during that period.

explicit_text: I spent my first six weeks in the Army at Wakeman General Hospital, which was part of Camp Atterbury. I was there recovering from a crushed right big toe caused by a piano accident during a detail. The hospital cared for many wounded soldiers from the war, and I also used the time to get dental work done since my family couldn’t afford it before.

explicit_text: After recovering at Camp Atterbury, I was sent to Fort Eustis, Virginia, for basic training with the Transportation Corps. I spent about two and a half to three months there, from late 1946 until December, learning everything from marching and rifle training to vehicle recovery. The living conditions were good, with barracks housing about 24 men per floor and a structured daily routine.

explicit_text: I was assigned to the Transportation Corps during my basic training at Fort Eustis. The training included learning how to operate and recover vehicles, rifle and sub-machine gun training, obstacle courses, and physical fitness. This unit prepared me for the logistics and transport roles I would fulfill in the Army.

explicit_text: Pike's Peak came up during a motorcycle ride I took with my cousin, which was one of my first longer trips away from home before joining the Army. It was a memorable experience because I had never been away from home for more than a week before that. This ride helped me get used to being away from Rossford before my military service.
